sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 64, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Level 1 has a blue background with a light blue vertical divider strip, a yellow right-edge strip, and a black bottom bar. Three "F"-like shapes exist: (1) a black F-shape on the left containing 5 white dot children, (2) a charcoal F-shape on the right, and (3) a yellow F-shape at bottom-right. The white dots appear to be interactive elements. No clear player avatar visible.
- Goal model: Click on the F-shapes or white dots with MOUSE before the timer runs out. The white dots inside the black F-shape are likely targets.
- Action model: Arrow keys, SPACE, MOUSE, and ACTION7 available. Need to probe what they do.
- Plan: Move cursor left with many LEFT presses, then SPACE, to see if a mark appears away from the timer strip.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 63 actions; a strong score needs about 32 or fewer. You are 2.0x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! SPACE didn't complete the level. The dots remain: 3 yellow and 2 white. The board didn't change except for the timer advancing.
Let me reconsider the goal. The dots are:
- 3 yellow dots at row 46, cols 52, 55, 58 (top row of the F-shape)
- 2 white dots at rows 49, 52, col 58 (right side of the F-shape)
The yellow dots are where the black F-shape overlaps the yellow F-shape. The white dots are where the black F-shape doesn't overlap the yellow F-shape.
Maybe the goal is to position the black F-shape so ALL 5 dots overlap with the yellow F-shape (turning all of them yellow)? Currently only 3 are yellow.
Looking at the F-shape structure:
- The black F-shape has a top bar (rows 45-47) and a vertical stem (rows 45-53, right side)
- The yellow F-shape has the same structure
- The top bar dots (3 dots) are yellow because they overlap
- The stem dots (2 dots) are white because they don't overlap
Wait, but the black F and yellow F are at the same position (cols 51-59, rows 45-53). So they should fully overlap. Unless the F-shapes have different orientations...
Let me reconsider. The black F-shape has the stem on the RIGHT side (cols 57-59), and the yellow F-shape might have the stem on the LEFT side (cols 51-53). If they're mirror images, the top bars would overlap but the stems would be on opposite sides!
Looking at the image:
- The black F-shape outline is visible with dots inside
- The yellow F-shape is visible behind it
- The top row dots (3 dots) are yellow
- The right-side dots (2 dots) are white
This suggests the black F-shape's stem is on the right, and the yellow F-shape's stem is on the left. The top bars overlap (hence yellow dots), but the stems don't overlap (hence white dots on the right).
So the goal might be to:
1. Flip the black F-shape somehow
2. Or move it so the stems overlap
3. Or position it so all dots turn yellow
Wait, maybe I need to use the charcoal F-shape too! The charcoal F-shape is at cols 3-11, rows 45-53. It's a mirror of the black F-shape. If I could somehow combine them...
Actually, let me reconsider the whole game. Maybe the goal is:
1. Move the black F-shape to overlap the yellow F-shape
2. The dots that overlap turn yellow
3. The goal is to have ALL dots turn yellow
4. But since the F-shapes are mirror images, the stems don't overlap
5. Maybe I need to move the black F-shape so its stem aligns with the yellow F-shape's stem
If the yellow F-shape has its stem on the LEFT (cols 51-53), and the black F-shape has its stem on the RIGHT (cols 57-59), then I need to shift the black F-shape left by 6 columns (2 LEFT actions) so its stem (now at cols 55-57) overlaps with... no, that still wouldn't work.
Wait, let me think about this differently. The F-shapes might not be mirror images. Let me check the original positions:
- Black F-shape originally at cols 15-23
- Charcoal F-shape originally at cols 36-44
- Yellow F-shape at cols 51-59
The black F-shape boundary was [[15, 15], [15, 23], [23, 23], [23, 21], [18, 21], [17, 20], [17, 15]].
The charcoal F-shape boundary was [[15, 36], [15, 44], [17, 44], [17, 39], [18, 38], [23, 38], [23, 36]].
Let me decode these:
Black F: 
- Top bar: rows 15-17, cols 15-23
- Stem: rows 17-23, cols 21-23 (right side)
So the black F has the stem on the RIGHT.
Charcoal F:
- Top bar: rows 15-17, cols 36-44
- Stem: rows 18-23, cols 36-38 (left side)
So the charcoal F has the stem on the LEFT.
They ARE mirror images! The black F has stem on right, charcoal F has stem on left.
Now, the yellow F-shape: looking at the image, it appears to have the stem on the LEFT (like the charcoal F). If the black F-shape (stem on right) overlaps the yellow F-shape (stem on left), the top bars overlap but stems don't.
So to make all dots yellow, I need to either:
1. Flip the black F-shape (is there a way to do this?)
2. Use the charcoal F-shape instead (stem on left, would overlap with yellow F)
3. Move the black F-shape to a different position where all dots overlap
Wait, maybe the goal isn't to make all dots yellow. Maybe the goal is something else entirely.
Let me reconsider. What if the goal is to:
1. Move the charcoal F-shape (which has stem on left, matching the yellow F) to overlap the yellow F-shape
2. The charcoal F-shape doesn't have white dots, so maybe it's the "correct" shape
Currently, the charcoal F-shape is at cols 3-11. I need to move it to cols 51-59. That's 48 columns right, which is 16 RIGHT actions. But RIGHT moves the charcoal F LEFT (since it moves away from the black F). So I need to use LEFT to move the charcoal F right!
Each LEFT moves the charcoal F right by 3. From col 3 to col 51 is 48 columns, which is 16 LEFT actions.
But LEFT also moves the black F left. The black F is at cols 51-59. 16 LEFT actions would move it to cols 51-48=3 to 59-48=11. So the black F would end up where the charcoal F currently is, and the charcoal F would end up where the black F currently is. They swap positions!
That's interesting but probably not the goal.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe the goal involves both F-shapes. What if I need to:
1. Move the black F to the yellow F position
2. Move the charcoal F to some other position
3. Press SPACE
Or maybe the goal is to have the two F-shapes (black and charcoal) overlap each other at the divider, creating a combined pattern.
Actually, let me re-examine the initial state. The F-shapes started at:
- Black F: cols 15-23 (left of divider)
- Charcoal F: cols 36-44 (right of divider)
- Divider: cols 30-32
They were symmetrically placed around the divider. Maybe the goal is to return them to their original positions? But that seems unlikely since I've already moved them around.
Let me try a different approach. What if I need to use ACTION7 or MOUSE? I haven't tried those yet.
Actually, let me try pressing SPACE a few more times to see if the timer reaching a certain value completes the level. Or maybe I need to move the F-shapes to a specific position and then press SPACE.
Wait, I just realized something. The timer (black strip at top-right) is growing, and the yellow strip is shrinking. Maybe the yellow strip represents remaining moves or time. When it reaches 0, the level fails. Currently it's at 21 pixels.
Let me try a completely different approach. What if the goal is to make the F-shapes match a pattern? The yellow F-shape might be the target pattern, and I need to position the black F-shape (with its dots) so that the dots align with specific parts of the yellow F-shape.
Looking at the current state:
- 3 yellow dots (overlapping with yellow F)
- 2 white dots (not overlapping)
Maybe I need to shift the black F-shape so the 2 white dots also overlap. Since the stems are on opposite sides, I need to shift the black F left by 6 columns (so its right stem moves to where the yellow F's left stem is). That's 2 LEFT actions.
Let me try that!

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: The black F-shape overlaps the yellow F, but only 3 of 5 dots turned yellow (top bar). The 2 stem dots remain white because the stems are on opposite sides (black F stem on right, yellow F stem on left). I need to shift the black F left so its stem overlaps the yellow F's stem.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Black F stem is on right (cols 57-59), yellow F stem is on left (cols 51-53)
# Need to shift black F left by 6 cols (2 LEFT actions) to align stems
# But LEFT moves black F left AND charcoal F right

predict(expect_change=True, note='LEFT x2 shifts black F stem to overlap yellow F stem')
action(['LEFT'] * 2)

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After LEFT x2:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] <= 5:
        print(f"  Dot: color={n['color']}, pos={n['boundary']}")
    if n['color'] in ['B', 'c', 'Y'] and n['pixels'] > 20:
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"  {n['color']}: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")

# Try SPACE
predict(expect_change=True, note='SPACE to test if all dots yellow completes level')
action(['SPACE'])

print(f"\nResult: level_completed={last_action_result.get('level_completed')}, done={last_action_result.get('done')}")

seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg2['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] == 1:
        print(f"  Dot: color={n['color']}, pos={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
